contentSlot: Thursday — psychology or named human phenomenon
unavailableProviders: vidIQ MCP DISCONNECTED this session — no Instagram/TikTok/YouTube outlier sweep, so the elite gate's outlierEvidence leg cannot be scored from live creator data. Scores below leave outlierEvidence UNSCORED rather than invented; these are RESEARCH candidates, not gate-passed.

candidates.topic: Some people cannot picture an apple — their mind's eye is blank
candidates.frameZero: PICTURE AN APPLE. SOME PEOPLE SEE NOTHING.
candidates.payoff: About 1 in 25 people have no mind's eye at all. Most never find out, because nobody describes imagining out loud.
candidates.whyNow: The 'apple test' spreads virally on its own because it makes the viewer TEST THEMSELVES mid-video — the playbook's strongest confirmed retention mechanism (participation beats passive explanation).
candidates.visualProof: The 5-step apple-vividness scale is a clean original diagram we can draw ourselves — no licensing, no AI-human risk.
candidates.productionRisk: LOW. Original graphics only. Must say 'about 1-4%' (estimate, not a hard number) and attribute the term to Zeman 2015.

evidence.note: Zeman coined 'aphantasia' 2015 (Univ. of Exeter), VVIQ-based; prevalence est. 1-4%
apple test spreads as a self-test format across social

candidates.topic: Things you did many times feel like they happened longer ago
candidates.payoff: Your sense of how long ago something happened is built from novelty, not the calendar — so a routine you repeated for years feels ancient.
candidates.whyNow: Published 2025 in Psychological Science and robustly replicated across samples; it REVERSES the standard model (stronger memory should feel more recent).
candidates.visualProof: Abstract — time perception has no photographable subject. WEAKEST leg; would need a designed diagram.
candidates.productionRisk: MEDIUM: visual proof must be invented, and doctrine 18 warns against diagram/footage medium mismatch.

evidence.note: Sherman & Yousif, 'An Illusion of Time Caused by Repeated Experience', Psychological Science 2025

candidates.topic: The urge to jump from a high place means the opposite of what people think
candidates.frameZero: THAT URGE ISN'T A DEATH WISH
candidates.payoff: Researchers call it the high place phenomenon: a fast safety signal your conscious mind misreads. The urge to jump affirms the urge to live.
candidates.whyNow: Hames et al. 2012 (n=431), replicated in two German samples (BMC Psychiatry 2020) and a 2025 clinical follow-up. Reassuring, highly transferable payoff.
candidates.visualProof: Strong: real licensed high-place/edge footage, no humans required.
candidates.productionRisk: HIGH — SELF-HARM ADJACENCY. Platform self-harm policies are strict and could suppress or flag it, and a vulnerable viewer could be harmed by careless framing. NOT recommended without deliberate duty-of-care review, despite the science being solid and the payoff genuinely reassuring.

evidence.note: Hames et al. 2012, J. Affective Disorders, n=431
